issue: Role and significance of nuclear weapons in military and security concepts, doctrines and policies

entries.position_summary: The United States defends extended deterrence arrangements as consistent with NPT Articles I and II, emphasizing that it does not transfer nuclear weapons or control to allies and does not assist NNWS in acquiring them. It argues such alliances reinforce the NPT by reducing incentives for allies to develop their own nuclear weapons.
entries.quote: Finally, Chair, I wish to highlight what we can only see as a deliberate effort to mischaracterize extended deterrence arrangements. It seems that some here have conveniently forgotten that the NPT was negotiated with a clear acknowledgment of nuclear alliances, and for the express purpose of ensuring there is no — that is, zero — proliferation of nuclear weapons through such alliances. In fact, such alliances reinforce the NPT by reducing the incentive for allied states to develop or acquire nuclear weapons on their own. U.S. nuclear deterrence relationships operate in strict conformity with Articles I and II of the NPT. We do not and will not transfer nuclear weapons or control over nuclear weapons to anyone, and we do not and will not in any way assist or encourage any non-nuclear weapon State to acquire nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: Citing its 2022 Nuclear Posture Review, the country aims to balance deterrence requirements with steps to reduce the role and salience of nuclear weapons in its national security strategy. It supports lowering reliance on nuclear weapons in military doctrine.
entries.quote: As articulated in the 2022 Nuclear Posture Review, we fully recognize the enduring importance of a nuclear policy that balances the evolving demands of deterrence with our goal of taking steps to reduce the role of nuclear weapons in our national security strategy, thereby also reducing the salience of nuclear weapons globally.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms a negative security assurance: it will not use or threaten to use nuclear weapons against NPT non-nuclear-weapon states that are in compliance with their obligations. Framed in its Nuclear Posture Review, this doctrine is intended to underscore the security benefits of NPT adherence and full compliance.
entries.quote: Moreover, as reaffirmed in our Nuclear Posture Review, the United States maintains a negative security assurance that it will not use or threaten to use nuclear weapons against non-nuclear weapons states that are party to the NPT and are in compliance with their nuclear non-proliferation obligations. This policy applies to the vast majority of countries. Our policy underscores the security benefits of adherence to the NPT and full compliance with respective nuclear

entries.position_summary: The United States defends extended deterrence and nuclear-sharing arrangements as consistent with the NPT, emphasizing that the U.S. retains full control of its nuclear weapons and upholds the highest safety and security standards. It argues these alliances provide credible security assurances that deter allies from seeking their own nuclear weapons and enhance overall stability, rejecting criticisms as false narratives and historical revisionism.
entries.quote: Chair, Once again, the United States reaffirms its commitment to nuclear nonprolifera�on and has been a leading actor in addressing the prolifera�on threats of today. Unfortunately, some false narra�ves have persisted during the current review cycle, o�en originally stemming from two countries who have not shown the same commitment to nonprolifera�on. As a result, some States Par�es are now mischaracterizing extended deterrence arrangements. To be absolutely clear, the United States retains complete control of its nuclear weapons and applies the highest safety and security standards under these extended deterrence arrangements. The NPT was nego�ated with a clear understanding of nuclear alliances because they predate the NPT. In fact, such arrangements have strengthened global nonprolifera�on providing allies with the necessary security assurances to ensure they do not feel the need to develop na�onal nuclear weapon capabili�es. These defensive alliances do not only benefit their members; they also increase stability. All na�ons benefit from protec�on against an unstable world. We encourage States Par�es to reject historical revisionism about extended deterrence.

entries.position_summary: The U.S. says the fundamental role of its nuclear weapons is to deter nuclear attack against itself, its allies, and partners, reflecting extended deterrence and a restrained role. It would consider nuclear use only in extreme circumstances to defend vital interests, indicating it does not adopt a no-first-use pledge. It rejects threatening nuclear use for intimidation or expansionist aims.
entries.quote: As long as nuclear weapons exist, the fundamental role of U.S. nuclear weapons is to deter nuclear attack on the United States, our allies, and our partners. The United States would consider the use of nuclear weapons only in extreme circumstances to defend the vital interests of the United States or its allies and partners. Unlike some others, we will not threaten the use of nuclear weapons to intimidate or to pursue an expansionist security policy.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency through its NPR and stockpile disclosures, reports steep unilateral reductions including in non-strategic weapons, and highlights measures lowering operational status and the role of nuclear weapons in its strategy. It encourages China and Russia to adopt similar transparency. This is presented as part of fulfilling disarmament commitments and advancing risk reduction.
entries.quote: well as in the public release of information in our Nuclear Posture Review and pertaining to our nuclear weapons stockpile. The U.S. national report to the 2022 RevCon also provided detailed information on other relevant topics, including steep U.S. reductions in non-strategic nuclear weapons and other unilateral cuts beyond treaty-based reductions over the past three decades, significant measures taken to reduce operational status of nuclear weapons systems, and the reduced role of nuclear weapons in U.S. national security strategy, including with respect to the 2022 NPR’s Declaratory Policy. We encourage the PRC and Russia to provide similar transparency.

entries.position_summary: The United States criticizes China’s rapid, opaque expansion of its nuclear arsenal, citing new ICBM silos, delivery systems, and ongoing production of fissile material, with projections of 1,000+ warheads by 2030. It argues China’s buildup contradicts its stated policies of minimum deterrence and No First Use and contrasts with other NPT nuclear-weapon states’ behavior. The U.S. asserts it acts responsibly as a nuclear-weapon state under the NPT.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, the United States is committed to responsible behavior as a nuclear-weapon State under the NPT. Not all NPT nuclear-weapon States have done the same. We continue to be deeply concerned by China’s rapid, opaque, and destabilizing expansion of its nuclear arsenal, including the construction of hundreds of new ICBM silos and the development of new delivery systems. We assess that China currently has 600 operational nuclear weapons, which will increase to over 1,000 by the year 2030, and that it is actively producing new fissile material for use in its growing nuclear arsenal, unlike the other four nuclear-weapon states. China’s activities indicate it seeks the ability to inflict far greater levels of overwhelming damage to an adversary in a nuclear exchange than it has historically deemed necessary, and all while professing to apply “minimum deterrence” and “No First Use” in its nuclear authorities.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear build-up, questioning the credibility and clarity of its no-first-use policy and broader doctrine. It notes Beijing’s reluctance to engage substantively and expresses skepticism about a proposed no-first-use treaty, particularly regarding how it would be verified. Overall, it views China’s initiative as lacking substance.
entries.quote: The PRC’s actions, in particular its rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up, raise questions about Beijing’s already ambiguous, stated “no first use” policy and its nuclear doctrine more broadly, calling into question what such an initiative aims to achieve. The PRC, to date, has resisted substantive engagement to answer these questions. We also continue to have concerns about how its proposed no first use treaty would operate in practice, including with respect to verification. We see more style than substance.

entries.position_summary: The country condemns the DPRK’s escalatory rhetoric and missile activities, noting Pyongyang’s portrayal of launches as trial runs for tactical nuclear weapon use. It also criticizes the DPRK’s malicious cyber activities used to finance and advance its unlawful weapons programs.
entries.quote: 2022. Pyongyang’s rhetoric has become increasingly escalatory and aggressive, going so far as to characterize certain of its missile launches and other military activities as trial runs for the use of tactical nuclear weapons. And it continues to generate revenue through malicious cyber activities that target all countries to further advance its unlawful weapons programs.

entries.position_summary: The country reports an 88% reduction of its nuclear stockpile and seeks to further lessen the role of nuclear weapons in its defense strategy, while acknowledging current security conditions. This frames its actions as contributing to disarmament and provides a degree of transparency.
entries.quote: We have reduced the number of nuclear weapons in our stockpile by over 88% and, while fully cognizant of the current security environment, continue to seek opportunities to reduce the role of nuclear weapons in our national defense strategy.

entries.position_summary: The United States argues that Russia and China’s refusal to engage in arms control compels it and its allies to prepare for unconstrained nuclear competition. In such a context, the U.S. may reassess and potentially augment its nuclear capabilities and adjust its posture to address these threats.
entries.quote: However, in contrast to our positive approach and longstanding efforts to manage rivalry and unrestrained competition through arms control, the outright refusal of Russia and the PRC to even discuss arms control at this time obliges the United States and our close allies and partners to prepare for a world of nuclear competition without numerical constraints. In such a world, the United States might have to reconsider its capabilities and posture to account for the threats posed by Russia and the PRC.

entries.position_summary: It opposes the chair’s emphasis on NNWS reporting about nuclear burden sharing and extended deterrence, arguing these states remain fully NPT-compliant. It defends extended deterrence as enhancing international peace and security and supporting nonproliferation goals.
entries.quote: concerned that the chair’s paper placed an emphasis on nuclear burden sharing and extended deterrence arrangements in non-nuclear weapon State reporting. These are non-nuclear-weapon States in full compliance with their NPT obligations. Furthermore, extended deterrence arrangements have a proven record of enhancing international peace and security and contributing to our collective nonproliferation objectives.

entries.position_summary: The United States affirms that the President holds sole authority to employ nuclear weapons, clarifying centralized command-and-control in its nuclear doctrine. This statement contributes to transparency regarding U.S. nuclear decision-making processes.
entries.quote: Let me be clear, the President of the United States has the sole decision- making authority for U.S. nuclear weapon employment.

entries.position_summary: The statement endorses the principle that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought, emphasizing the need to ensure nuclear weapons are never used. It urges other nuclear-weapon states to uphold their 2022 reaffirmation of this risk-reduction principle.
entries.quote: President Trump has also spoken about the enormous power of nuclear weapons and the imperative to ensure there is never a need for such weapons to be used again, echoing President Reagan’s statement that “a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought.” We remind all other Nuclear Weapons States that they reaffirmed this principle in 2022.

entries.position_summary: The United States frames nuclear weapons primarily for deterrence, assurance of allies and partners, and potential use only if deterrence fails. It commits to maintaining a safe, secure, reliable, and effective nuclear deterrent as long as nuclear weapons exist, while stating a very high threshold for nuclear employment, limited to extreme circumstances.
entries.quote: The fundamental role of U.S. nuclear weapons is to deter a strategic attack, assure allies and partners, and achieve objectives if deterrence fails. The United States will continue to maintain a safe, secure, reliable, and effective nuclear deterrent as long as nuclear weapons exist. As has been made clear by successive U.S. Administrations, the United States maintains a very high bar for nuclear employment and would consider the use of nuclear weapons only in extreme circumstances. 1
